mnemonics.title: Hydrogen Production Methods
mnemonics.content: WAGS: Water Electrolysis (clean), Steam Methane Reforming (common), Biomass Gasification (renewable)

mnemonics.title: Hydrogen Color Codes
mnemonics.content: GREY = Gone (fossil, releases CO2), BLUE = Better (with carbon capture), GREEN = Great (renewable, zero emissions)

mnemonics.title: Fuel Cell Types
mnemonics.content: PEM: Proton Exchange Membrane - Perfect for Vehicles; SOFC: Solid Oxide Fuel Cells - Stationary power; AFC: Alkaline Fuel Cells - Space missions

acronyms.usage: Most common industrial hydrogen production method using natural gas
Most common fuel cell type for hydrogen vehicles
Technology to capture CO2 emissions in blue hydrogen production
Energy content of hydrogen when water remains as vapor (120 MJ/kg)
Emissions that hydrogen energy aims to reduce compared to fossil fuels
Chemical formula for diatomic hydrogen gas used as fuel

visualTips.visualization: Think of hydrogen as a 'battery' that stores energy - you put energy in (to produce it), then release that energy when used. Like charging a battery, but the 'charge' is hydrogen gas.

Imagine a sandwich: hydrogen on one side, oxygen on the other, with a special membrane in between. The membrane acts like a one-way door, letting only protons through, creating an electrical current - like a waterfall generating hydroelectric power.

visualTips.visualize: A balloon vs a tiny basketball - hydrogen at atmospheric pressure would need huge volume (balloon), but compressed (350 bar) or liquefied (-253°C), it fits into a small container like a basketball.
Hydrogen is like a feather that packs a punch - very light weight but releases lots of energy when burned, like comparing a small but powerful rocket to a big heavy truck.

analogies.analogy: Just as money transfers value between people, hydrogen transfers energy between sectors - producing it where renewable electricity is abundant, transporting it, and using it where needed.

A fuel cell is like a hydroelectric dam - water (hydrogen) flows through continuously generating power. A battery is like a reservoir - you fill it up, then use what's stored. Both generate electricity, but work differently.

Grey hydrogen is like buying products from a factory that pollutes. Blue hydrogen is like that factory installing filters. Green hydrogen is like buying from a solar-powered factory - no pollution at all.
Building hydrogen infrastructure today is like building roads in the early automobile era - expensive and sparse, but essential for the technology to become mainstream.
